Press enter or click to view image in full size

When I first started learning React, I thought:
“Okay… it’s just another JavaScript library that makes coding easier.”
I memorized useState, components, props, JSX...
But one question kept bothering me.
Why was React even created?
Then I discovered the story behind it and it was so interesting.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Imagine you’re using Facebook.
Someone likes your post.
A new notification arrives.
A friend sends you a message.
Today, these updates appear almost instantly.
But years ago, building interfaces like this wasn’t so smooth.
Facebook’s website had become huge, and updating the page efficiently was a real challenge. In many cases, changing a small piece of the interface could require much more work than necessary.
Facebook engineers wanted a better way. So around 2013, they released a JavaScript library called React.
And the idea behind it was surprisingly simple.
Instead of updating the whole page
React creates something called the Virtual DOM.
(DOM = Document Object Model: a tree-like structure that represents all the HTML elements on a web page, such as buttons, text, images, and forms.)
Think of the Virtual DOM as a lightweight copy of the real DOM.
Whenever your data changes, React first updates this virtual copy.
Then it compares the new version with the previous one, figures out exactly what changed, and updates only that specific part of the real web page.
Not the entire page. Just the part that actually changed.
Think of it like editing one word in a 500-page book. Would you print the entire book again? Of course not. You would replace only that one page.
That’s essentially how React treats your web page.
Why is this such a big deal?
Because it makes applications feel much faster.
Instead of constantly reloading everything, React updates only what’s necessary, that means:

• Faster UI updates

• Fewer unnecessary page refreshes

• Reusable components that can be used across your application

• Easier maintenance as projects grow

• A smoother experience for users
My biggest realization
Before learning the story, React felt like another technology I had to memorize. Now it feels like a clever solution to a real engineering problem.
That’s what makes programming so interesting.
Every technology exists because someone faced a problem and asked:
“Can we build a better way?”
React is one of those answers.
And somehow, knowing the why made learning the how much more enjoyable.

I’m still a beginner, but moments like this remind me that programming isn’t just about syntax.
It’s about solving problems creatively.
And that’s probably my favorite part of learning to code.
